descriptions.description: The Shenyang J-16 (Chinese: 歼-16; pinyin: Jian-Shiliu), also known as Qianlong (Chinese: 潜龙; lit. 'Hidden Dragon', NATO reporting name: Flanker-N) is a Chinese all-weather 4.5 generation, tandem-seat, twin-engine, multirole strike fighter built by Shenyang Aircraft Corporation and operated by the People's Liberation Army Air Force (PLAAF). It is developed from the Shenyang J-11, the licensed production variant of the Russian Sukhoi Su-27. Its design is based on the Su-30MKK.
descriptions.shortDescription: The Shenyang J-16 (Chinese: 歼-16; pinyin: Jian-Shiliu), also known as Qianlong (Chinese: 潜龙; lit.

descriptions.description: With the development of military aircraft during the turn of the century, the PLAAF found its JH-7 fighters becoming increasingly obsolete. In the 1990s, China purchased Sukhoi Su-27 and Sukhoi Su-30MKK air superiority fighters from Russia, including those license-produced in China as the Shenyang J-11A. The J-11A was further developed into the J-11B single-seat and BS twin-seat variant with indigenous technology. The J-16 is a strike fighter derived from the J-11BS model.
descriptions.shortDescription: With the development of military aircraft during the turn of the century, the PLAAF found its JH-7 fighters becoming increasingly obsolete.

descriptions.description:
The J-16 is equipped with an active electronically scanned array (AESA) radar and is powered by two Chinese Shenyang WS-10A turbofan engines. Weight is reduced through greater use of composite materials. J-16 units have received radar-absorbent paint to reduce radar signature, enhancing suppression of enemy air defenses (SEAD) capability in conjunction with electronic support measures pods. The cockpit is fitted with a helmet-mounted display (HMD) system to improve pilot situational awareness. An electronic warfare version, the J-16D, was developed in the 2010s, reportedly first flying in 2015; it is designed for SEAD, capable of housing internal jamming equipment and carrying external electronic warfare pods.

According to aviation researcher Justin Bronk of RUSI, the J-16 holds advantages over Russian Flanker variants through wide use of composite materials, longer-range missiles, and advanced sensors/avionics, representing a shift by Chinese industry away from dependence on Russian technology toward derivatives superior to the originals. British combat aviation expert Abraham Abrams called the J-16 'by far the world's most advanced Flanker derivative,' noting many of its technologies derive from the J-20 fifth-generation fighter program. In March 2021, a J-16 variant reportedly had its backseat co-pilot replaced by an AI algorithm called 'intelligence victory,' undergoing testing at Shenyang Aircraft Corporation, with a similar aircraft spotted near Malan, Xinjiang in June 2021.
descriptions.shortDescription: The J-16 is equipped with an active electronically scanned array (AESA) radar and is powered by two Chinese Shenyang WS-10A turbofan engines.

descriptions.description:
The J-16 fully replaced the single-seat J-11B in production in 2018, after being produced in parallel for over half a decade; a small number of J-11BS trainers continued production alongside J-16s. The first flight is believed to have occurred in 2011-2012. In April 2014, the PLAAF received a regiment of J-16s. The J-16 entered service in 2015 and was officially revealed in 2017 during the PLA's 90th anniversary parade. Production estimates: by 2022, at least 245 airframes produced; by end of 2023, more than 280; by August 2024, Military Watch Magazine estimated the fleet exceeded 350 fighters (13th production batch then in deliveries, each batch comprising 24-30 aircraft), making the J-16 'by far the most widely commissioned heavyweight fighter class by a single air force anywhere in the world since the turn of the century.' As of September 2025, open-source analysis of serial numbers (e.g., '1518') suggested at least 450 J-16s produced. In 2021, the Chinese Air Force began inducting the J-16D electronic warfare variant into combat training.

On 26 May 2022, a J-16 intercepted a RAAF P-8 Poseidon surveillance aircraft over the South China Sea, releasing flares and chaff, some of which were ingested by the P-8's engine; Australia lodged a protest while China accused the Australian pilots of acting 'dangerously and provocatively.' J-16 fighters are the most frequently used Chinese aircraft deployed in the Taiwan Strait, likely due to electronic warfare capabilities; large numbers were dispatched in August 2022 in response to Nancy Pelosi's Taiwan visit. On 26 May 2023, a J-16 conducted a fly-by cutting directly in front of a U.S. RC-135 Rivet Joint reconnaissance aircraft over the South China Sea, forcing it through jet wash ('thumping'); the U.S. called it an unnecessarily aggressive maneuver while China said the American aircraft had intruded into a Chinese military training area.

People's Liberation Army Air Force - approximately 330 J-16 and 20 J-16D as of 2026 (per The Military Balance 2026).
descriptions.shortDescription: The J-16 fully replaced the single-seat J-11B in production in 2018, after being produced in parallel for over half a decade; a small number of J-11BS trainers continued production alongside J-16s.
